places.name: Commander's Palace
places.reviewSummary: The grande dame of Creole fine dining in a turquoise Victorian, famous for turtle soup and 25-cent martinis at the legendary jazz brunch.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers rave about impeccable service and the bread pudding souffle, though reservations book weeks out and it isn't cheap.
places.localTip: Book the boozy weekend jazz brunch and order the 25-cent martinis (max three).
places.story: Since 1893 this turquoise-and-white landmark has crowned the Garden District, and it's where chefs Paul Prudhomme and Emeril Lagasse first earned their stripes. Ask for a table in the airy Garden Room, where live jazz drifts between courses of turtle soup laced with sherry.
places.highlight: Don't miss the bread pudding souffle

places.name: Parkway Bakery & Tavern
places.reviewSummary: A century-old neighborhood institution slinging some of the city's best fried-shrimp and roast-beef po'boys near Bayou St. John.
places.googleReviewSummary: Locals and visitors agree the gravy-drenched roast beef is unbeatable, but lines and limited seating can mean a wait.
places.localTip: Get the roast beef 'debris' po'boy 'dressed' and eat it on the bayou-side patio.
places.story: Parkway opened in 1911 and fed the workers digging the nearby canals; President Obama famously stopped in for a shrimp po'boy in 2010. The bread comes from Leidenheimer, the same flaky French loaf that's defined the New Orleans po'boy for over a century.

places.name: Cochon
places.reviewSummary: Chef Donald Link's love letter to Cajun cooking, all wood-fired pork, rabbit, and oyster-bacon roast in a buzzing Warehouse District room.
places.googleReviewSummary: Diners praise the fried boudin and pork cheeks as standouts, though some find the noise level high and portions rich.
places.localTip: Start with the wood-fired oysters and the fried boudin with pickled peppers.
places.story: Cochon means 'pig' in French, and chef Donald Link built this place around the whole-hog traditions of his rural Acadiana roots. The boucherie spirit runs deep here, where house-cured meats hang and everything that can be smoked, is.

places.name: Dong Phuong Bakery
places.address: 14207 Chef Menteur Hwy, New Orleans, LA 70129
places.reviewSummary: A James Beard-honored Vietnamese bakery in New Orleans East beloved for banh mi and the city's most coveted Mardi Gras king cake.
places.googleReviewSummary: Fans call the banh mi and king cake the best in the city, but it's a drive from downtown and king cake season sells out fast.
places.localTip: Order the BBQ pork banh mi; in Carnival season, pre-order the king cake online days ahead.
places.story: Founded by Vietnamese refugees in 1982, Dong Phuong supplies French bread to half the po'boy shops in town. Its cream-cheese king cake became such a phenomenon that the bakery won a James Beard America's Classic award in 2018.
places.highlight: Don't miss the king cake in Carnival season

places.name: Casamento's Restaurant
places.reviewSummary: A tiled century-old oyster house famous for raw oysters on the half shell and the singular fried-oyster 'loaf' on pan bread.
places.googleReviewSummary: Regulars love the spotless gleaming tile and fresh oysters, but note it closes for summer and takes cash preferentially.
places.localTip: Order the oyster loaf on the house-made 'pan bread' and check hours — they close in summer.
places.story: Casamento's has shucked oysters since 1919 inside a gleaming room tiled floor-to-ceiling so it could be scrubbed spotless every night. The family famously shuts down during the warm months when local oysters aren't at their best — a stubborn old-world commitment to quality.

places.name: Dooky Chase's Restaurant
places.reviewSummary: The legendary Treme dining room of the late Leah Chase, where civil-rights leaders strategized over gumbo z'herbes and fried chicken.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are moved by the history and the gumbo, though the buffet-style lunch hours are limited and it's often busy.
places.localTip: Come for the weekday Creole lunch buffet and order the gumbo z'herbes if it's on.
places.story: Leah Chase, the 'Queen of Creole Cuisine,' turned this restaurant into a meeting ground where Black and white leaders broke segregation laws to plan the civil-rights movement over her cooking. Her art-filled dining room hosted everyone from Thurgood Marshall to two sitting U.S. presidents.
places.highlight: Don't miss the fried chicken

places.name: Antoine's Restaurant
places.address: 713 St Louis St, New Orleans, LA 70130
places.reviewSummary: America's oldest family-run restaurant, birthplace of Oysters Rockefeller, sprawling through 14 ornate French Quarter dining rooms.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the old-world grandeur and tableside theatrics, but a few find the cooking traditional and the rooms maze-like.
places.localTip: Ask for a tour of the back dining rooms and the Mystery Room, and order Oysters Rockefeller where they were invented.
places.story: Founded in 1840, Antoine's invented Oysters Rockefeller in 1899 — named for the richness of its secret green sauce. Its labyrinth of private rooms holds Mardi Gras krewe memorabilia and a wine cellar with a quarter-million bottles.

places.name: Willie Mae's Scotch House
places.address: 2401 St Ann St, New Orleans, LA 70119
places.reviewSummary: A James Beard-honored Treme cottage serving what many call America's best fried chicken, crisp and brined to perfection.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers swear by the crackly fried chicken, though seating is tight and lines form well before opening.
places.localTip: Arrive right at opening — the line gets long fast and they can sell out.
places.story: Willie Mae Seaton opened this neighborhood spot in 1957 and her secret wet-battered recipe earned a James Beard 'America's Classic' award. After Hurricane Katrina flooded the building, volunteers from across the country rebuilt it brick by brick.
places.highlight: Don't miss the fried chicken

places.name: Cafe du Monde
places.reviewSummary: The iconic 24-hour open-air coffee stand by the river, serving only beignets and chicory cafe au lait since 1862.
places.googleReviewSummary: An essential New Orleans rite that everyone loves for the powdered-sugar beignets, though crowds and long lines are constant.
places.localTip: Skip the worst lines by going late at night — it's open 24 hours; bring cash and don't wear black.
places.story: Cafe du Monde has poured chicory coffee at the foot of the French Market since 1862, a habit New Orleanians picked up during Civil War coffee shortages. The menu has never changed: just three beignets dusted in a snowstorm of powdered sugar and a cup of cafe au lait.
places.highlight: Don't miss the order of three beignets

places.name: French Truck Coffee
places.reviewSummary: A bright, locally roasted specialty coffee bar that helped lead New Orleans' third-wave coffee scene.
places.googleReviewSummary: Patrons love the fresh-roasted beans and cheerful yellow space, though seating fills up and parking is tight.
places.localTip: Grab a cold brew and a pastry, then walk the Magazine Street shops next door.
places.story: French Truck started as a tiny roastery obsessed with fresh beans, refusing to sell coffee more than a few weeks past roast. Its sunny yellow flagship on Magazine became a daily ritual for the Lower Garden District crowd.

places.name: Cafe Beignet on Royal
places.reviewSummary: A charming courtyard cafe serving beignets and breakfast with live jazz, a calmer alternative to its famous rival.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests enjoy the relaxed courtyard and live music with their beignets, though prices run higher than the no-frills competition.
places.localTip: Sit in the leafy back courtyard and catch the morning jazz trio.
places.story: Tucked into a historic Royal Street building once home to a police station, this cafe pairs powdered beignets with a serene fountain courtyard. It's the spot locals send friends who want the beignet experience without the Cafe du Monde crush.

places.name: La Boulangerie
places.reviewSummary: An authentic French neighborhood bakery on Magazine Street turning out flaky croissants, baguettes, and quiche.
places.googleReviewSummary: Regulars praise the buttery croissants and genuine French feel, though the small space gets crowded on weekend mornings.
places.localTip: Get there early for the almond croissants before they sell out.
places.story: Part of chef Donald Link's restaurant family, La Boulangerie bakes in the old-world French tradition that shaped New Orleans' bread culture. The ham-and-cheese croissant has a cult following among Uptown locals.

places.name: CC's Coffee House (Esplanade)
places.reviewSummary: A beloved Louisiana-born coffeehouse chain famous for the Mochasippi blended iced coffee.
places.googleReviewSummary: Locals appreciate the comfy neighborhood vibe and Mochasippi, though Wi-Fi seats fill with students.
places.localTip: Order a Mochasippi — the frozen coffee drink that locals grew up on.
places.story: CC's grew out of the Community Coffee family, a Louisiana coffee dynasty dating to 1919. Its laid-back cafes became the unofficial living rooms of New Orleans neighborhoods long before national chains arrived.

places.name: The Vintage
places.reviewSummary: A stylish all-day cafe and wine bar known for beignets, charcuterie, and a buzzy happy hour.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the chic vibe and bottomless-mimosa brunch, though it can get loud and busy at peak hours.
places.localTip: Come for the boozy weekend brunch and the made-to-order beignets.
places.story: The Vintage reinvented the New Orleans coffeehouse as a day-to-night hangout where espresso gives way to natural wine after dark. Its modern beignets, dusted and stuffed, became an Instagram sensation on Magazine Street.

places.name: Jackson Square
places.reviewSummary: The historic heart of the French Quarter, a landscaped park framed by St. Louis Cathedral and ringed by artists and street performers.
places.googleReviewSummary: Everyone calls it the soul of the Quarter for its architecture and buskers, though it gets crowded and street vendors are persistent.
places.localTip: Visit at golden hour when the cathedral glows and the tarot readers set up along the fence.
places.story: Once a military parade ground called the Place d'Armes, this square is where Louisiana was formally transferred to the United States in the 1803 Louisiana Purchase. At its center rides Andrew Jackson on a bronze horse, one of the first equestrian statues cast in America.
places.highlight: Don't miss the artists' fence gallery along the perimeter

places.name: St. Louis Cathedral
places.address: 615 Pere Antoine Alley, New Orleans, LA 70116
places.reviewSummary: The triple-spired cathedral towering over Jackson Square, the oldest continuously active cathedral in the United States.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors find the interior serene and the facade stunning, though services limit access and it can be busy.
places.localTip: Step inside between masses to see the painted ceiling, then view it lit up at night from the square.
places.story: A church has stood on this spot since 1727, and the present cathedral's white spires have watched over the French Quarter since the 1850s. Behind it lies St. Anthony's Garden, where a statue of Jesus casts a dramatic 'touchdown' shadow at night.

places.name: French Market
places.address: 1008 N Peters St, New Orleans, LA 70116
places.reviewSummary: A six-block open-air market dating to 1791, mixing produce stalls, flea-market finds, and Creole food vendors along the river.
places.googleReviewSummary: Shoppers enjoy the history and local crafts, though some stalls feel touristy and prices vary widely.
places.localTip: Hit the food court for a praline and a cup of gumbo, then browse the flea market end.
places.story: The French Market claims to be the oldest public market in the country, trading on this riverside spot since 1791 when it was a Native American bartering ground. Generations of Creole, French, and Sicilian vendors have hawked everything here from gator-on-a-stick to Mardi Gras masks.

places.name: St. Charles Avenue Streetcar
places.address: St Charles Ave & Carondelet St, New Orleans, LA 70130
places.reviewSummary: The world's oldest continuously operating streetcar line, rumbling past mansions and live oaks down St. Charles Avenue.
places.googleReviewSummary: Riders love the nostalgic olive-green cars and Garden District views, though they're slow and crowded at peak times.
places.localTip: Bring exact change ($1.25) and ride the full line to Audubon Park for the best mansions.
places.story: These olive-green cars have rolled since 1835, making this the oldest streetcar line still running anywhere on Earth. The mahogany seats and brass fittings haven't changed in generations, and the open windows are the only air-conditioning you'll need.
places.highlight: Don't miss the oak-canopied stretch past the Garden District

places.name: The National WWII Museum
places.reviewSummary: America's official WWII museum, a sprawling, immersive campus consistently ranked among the best museums in the world.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are blown away by the immersive exhibits and Tom Hanks film, though it easily takes a full day and tickets add up.
places.localTip: Buy timed tickets online and budget at least four hours; the 'Beyond All Boundaries' film is worth the upgrade.
places.story: New Orleans hosts the national WWII museum because the Higgins boats that stormed Normandy's beaches were designed and built right here in the city. The dog-tag experience lets you follow one real soldier's journey through the entire war.
places.highlight: Don't miss the 'Beyond All Boundaries' 4D film

places.name: Steamboat Natchez
places.reviewSummary: An authentic steam-powered paddlewheeler offering Mississippi River cruises with live jazz and a calliope serenade.
places.googleReviewSummary: Passengers love the calliope music and river breeze, though some say the buffet is just okay and decks get crowded.
places.localTip: Take the daytime jazz cruise and tip the calliope player to hear a request as you depart.
places.story: The Natchez is the last authentic steamboat on the Mississippi, powered by engines that once drove a 1925 paddlewheeler. Before each cruise, its steam calliope wheezes out tunes that echo across the whole French Quarter.

places.name: St. Louis Cemetery No. 1
places.reviewSummary: The oldest surviving New Orleans cemetery, a maze of above-ground tombs including the reputed grave of Voodoo Queen Marie Laveau.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors find the tombs hauntingly beautiful, but access now requires a licensed tour guide and it's hot with little shade.
places.localTip: You must book a licensed guided tour to enter — go in the morning before the heat.
places.story: Opened in 1789, these 'cities of the dead' bury people above ground because the water table sits so high that coffins once floated away. Voodoo Queen Marie Laveau is said to rest here, and visitors long marked her tomb with three X's for a wish.

places.name: City Park
places.reviewSummary: A 1,300-acre park bigger than Central Park, home to ancient oaks, the sculpture garden, and the storybook Carousel Gardens.
places.googleReviewSummary: Families love the giant oaks and amusement rides, though it's spread out and some areas need a car to reach.
places.localTip: Walk beneath the centuries-old Singing Oak with its wind chimes, then grab beignets at Morning Call inside the park.
places.story: City Park holds one of the largest stands of mature live oaks in the world, some trees nearly 800 years old. The antique wooden carousel, built in 1906, is one of only a hundred such 'flying horses' left in the country.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Besthoff Sculpture Garden

places.name: The Bywater American Bistro
places.reviewSummary: Chef Nina Compton's casual Bywater bistro blending Caribbean flavors with Louisiana ingredients in a converted rail building.
places.googleReviewSummary: Diners rave about the inventive pasta and curry goat, though the small dining room books up and parking is street-only.
places.localTip: Order the curry goat and the jerk corn; reservations are smart on weekends.
places.story: St. Lucia-born chef Nina Compton, a 'Top Chef' favorite, opened this neighborhood spot to cook the Caribbean-Creole food of her upbringing. It sits in the gritty-artsy Bywater, a downriver neighborhood of shotgun houses and muralled walls.

places.name: Hansen's Sno-Bliz
places.reviewSummary: A James Beard-honored snowball stand serving feather-fine shaved ice with house-made syrups since 1939.
places.googleReviewSummary: Locals call it the gold standard of snowballs, but it's seasonal, cash-only, and lines stretch down the block in summer.
places.localTip: Get the cream of nectar with condensed milk on top; it's cash-only and summer-only.
places.story: Ernest Hansen invented his ice-shaving machine in 1939, and his granddaughter still runs the stand by hand today. The ice comes out so fine it feels like fresh snow, which is why this humble shack won a James Beard 'America's Classic' award.
places.highlight: Don't miss the cream of nectar

places.name: Vaughan's Lounge
places.reviewSummary: A beloved Bywater corner dive famous for raucous Thursday-night brass and red beans cooked up by the band.
places.googleReviewSummary: Regulars love the sweaty, authentic music nights, though it's a true dive — cash, no frills, and packed when bands play.
places.localTip: Come on a music night for the free red beans and rice the band sometimes serves.
places.story: This corner barroom became legendary as trumpeter Kermit Ruffins' Thursday-night home, where he'd barbecue out back between sets. It's the kind of unpretentious neighborhood joint where the music feels like a house party.

places.name: Domilise's Po-Boys
places.reviewSummary: A century-old po'boy shack in an Uptown house, beloved for fried shrimp and roast beef served over the bar.
places.googleReviewSummary: Fans cherish the no-frills authenticity and generous po'boys, though it's cash-only with limited hours and seating.
places.localTip: Get the half-shrimp, half-roast-beef po'boy and a Barq's root beer; bring cash.
places.story: Domilise's has served po'boys from this corner house since the 1920s, the recipes passed down through four generations of the family. There's no sign you'd notice — just a screen door, a tiny bar, and some of the best fried shrimp in the city.

places.name: Bacchanal Wine
places.reviewSummary: A backyard wine garden in the Bywater where you pick a bottle inside and listen to live jazz under string lights.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests adore the magical courtyard and live music, though waits for tables and the order system can frustrate.
places.localTip: Pick a bottle from the shop inside, then snag a courtyard table before the evening band starts.
places.story: Bacchanal began as a humble wine shop that started letting customers drink in the backyard, and the backyard became the whole point. Now it's a candle-lit garden of mismatched tables where world-class musicians play just feet from your bottle.
places.highlight: Don't miss the rooftop deck at sunset

places.name: Cure
places.reviewSummary: A James Beard-winning craft cocktail bar in a former firehouse that sparked the revival of the Freret Street corridor.
places.googleReviewSummary: Cocktail lovers praise the inventive, balanced drinks and elegant room, though prices are higher and it draws a crowd.
places.localTip: Sit at the bar and let the bartenders build you something off-menu to taste.
places.story: When Cure opened in a renovated 1905 firehouse in 2009, it single-handedly turned a forgotten Freret Street into a dining destination. It went on to win a James Beard award for outstanding bar program, putting New Orleans cocktail craft on the national map.

places.name: Jean Lafitte Swamp Tours
places.address: 6601 Leo Kerner Lafitte Pkwy, Marrero, LA 70072
places.reviewSummary: A small-boat ride through cypress-draped bayous south of the city, with alligators, herons, and Spanish moss.
places.googleReviewSummary: Tour-goers love the up-close gators and knowledgeable guides, though it's a drive out and gators are scarce in cold months.
places.localTip: Book a morning tour in warm months when alligators are most active; bring bug spray.
places.story: Just south of the city, the bayous open into a primeval world of cypress knees and Spanish moss where the Cajun fishing culture still thrives. Guides whose families have fished these waters for generations call the gators right up to the boat.
places.highlight: Don't miss the chance to hold a baby alligator

places.name: New Orleans School of Cooking
places.address: 524 St Louis St, New Orleans, LA 70130
places.reviewSummary: A hands-on and demo-style cooking experience teaching gumbo, jambalaya, and pralines in a historic French Quarter building.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the entertaining chefs and eating what's cooked, though demo classes are watch-only rather than hands-on.
places.localTip: Book the morning class so you're not too full for lunch elsewhere, and buy spices on the way out.
places.story: Set in a 1700s former molasses warehouse, this school has been demystifying Creole cooking for visitors since 1980. The chef-instructors lace every gumbo lesson with history, humor, and the holy trinity of onion, celery, and bell pepper.

places.name: Museum of the American Cocktail (Sazerac House)
places.reviewSummary: A free, three-floor interactive museum on Canal Street devoted to the Sazerac and New Orleans cocktail history, with tastings.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the free tastings and slick exhibits, though you must reserve and the samples are small.
places.localTip: Reserve a free timed ticket online; the self-guided tour includes complimentary cocktail and bitters tastings.
places.story: Housed in a restored Canal Street building, the Sazerac House celebrates the city's claim as birthplace of the cocktail itself. You can watch rye whiskey being distilled and sample the Sazerac, made with locally invented Peychaud's bitters.

places.name: French Quarter Phantoms Tours
places.address: 718 N Rampart St, New Orleans, LA 70116
places.reviewSummary: Highly rated walking tours covering the Quarter's ghosts, voodoo, vampires, and true-crime history with theatrical guides.
places.googleReviewSummary: Tour-takers consistently praise the entertaining, well-researched storytellers, though groups can be large in peak season.
places.localTip: Book the evening ghost-and-vampire tour and meet at the start point a few minutes early.
places.story: These guides weave the documented dark history of the Quarter — the LaLaurie mansion, yellow-fever plagues, and pirate hauntings — into theatrical night walks. They're storytellers first, blending fact and folklore the way New Orleans always has.

places.name: Royal Carriages
places.reviewSummary: Mule-drawn carriage tours departing Jackson Square for a clip-clop loop through the French Quarter's history.
places.googleReviewSummary: Riders enjoy the relaxed narrated loop and friendly mules, though some drivers' facts lean toward folklore.
places.localTip: Hire a private carriage rather than a shared one for a more personal, leisurely tour.
places.story: Mules, not horses, pull these carriages because they tolerate the Louisiana heat far better. The fringed buggies have circled Jackson Square for generations, narrating the Quarter at the gentle pace of hooves on cobblestone.

places.name: Old New Orleans Rum Distillery
places.reviewSummary: Louisiana's first post-Prohibition rum distillery, offering tours and tastings of cane-sugar rum founded by an artist.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the laid-back tour and generous tastings, though it's off the beaten path and tour times are limited.
places.localTip: Take the shuttle from the French Quarter if offered, since it's a haul from downtown.
places.story: Founded by artist James Michalopoulos in 1995, this distillery turns Louisiana sugarcane into rum the old Caribbean way. The tasting room doubles as a gallery, blending the founder's two passions — fine art and fine spirits.

places.name: New Orleans Museum of Art
places.address: 1 Collins C. Diboll Cir, New Orleans, LA 70124
places.reviewSummary: The city's premier art museum in a Beaux-Arts building in City Park, with strong French, African, and decorative arts.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors praise the collection and free sculpture garden, though the building is smaller than some expect.
places.localTip: Pair your visit with the adjacent free Besthoff Sculpture Garden across the lagoon.
places.story: NOMA opened in 1911 as the gift of a Jamaican-born sugar broker who wanted to bring great art to the city. Its galleries hold Faberge eggs, Degas paintings, and one of the South's finest collections of African art.

places.name: Sydney and Walda Besthoff Sculpture Garden
places.address: 1 Collins C. Diboll Cir, New Orleans, LA 70124
places.reviewSummary: A free, lushly landscaped garden of modern sculpture set among ancient oaks and lagoons beside NOMA.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests find it a peaceful, beautiful escape with major sculptures, though shade is limited on hot afternoons.
places.localTip: Go early or late to dodge the heat and catch soft light on the bronze works.
places.story: This free garden weaves more than ninety modern sculptures — Henry Moore, Louise Bourgeois, and more — through City Park's mossy oaks and pedestrian bridges. A 2019 expansion added a meandering footpath over the water, making art-viewing feel like a woodland stroll.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Spider sculpture by Louise Bourgeois

places.name: Contemporary Arts Center New Orleans
places.reviewSummary: A multidisciplinary contemporary art center anchoring the Warehouse District's gallery row with rotating exhibitions and performances.
places.googleReviewSummary: Art fans appreciate the bold rotating shows and performances, though exhibits change so offerings vary by visit.
places.localTip: Check the calendar for performance nights and visit during the monthly Arts District art walk.
places.story: Founded in 1976 in a converted warehouse, the CAC helped transform a derelict industrial district into New Orleans' Arts District. It champions living artists and experimental work, a deliberate counterpoint to the city's deep love of tradition.

places.name: House of Dance & Feathers
places.reviewSummary: A backyard museum in the Lower Ninth Ward preserving the culture of Mardi Gras Indians and social-aid clubs.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are deeply moved by the personal tour and beaded suits, though it's by appointment and hard to find.
places.localTip: Call ahead to arrange a visit — Ronald Lewis or family will give a personal tour.
places.story: Ronald Lewis built this museum in his own backyard to honor the Mardi Gras Indians and Social Aid and Pleasure Clubs of Black New Orleans. Inside are hand-sewn, feathered suits that take a year and thousands of beads to make.

places.name: Michalopoulos Gallery
places.reviewSummary: The French Quarter gallery of James Michalopoulos, whose swirling, vivid paintings of NOLA architecture define the city's look.
places.googleReviewSummary: Art lovers adore the vibrant, instantly recognizable cityscapes, though originals are expensive — prints are the affordable option.
places.localTip: Even if you can't buy an original, the prints and posters make the best NOLA souvenir.
places.story: Michalopoulos paints New Orleans' leaning shotgun houses and wrought-iron balconies in tilting, jewel-toned brushstrokes that capture the city's dreamlike feel. He's designed numerous Jazz Fest posters, making his style the unofficial visual signature of the festival.

places.name: A Gallery for Fine Photography
places.reviewSummary: A world-class fine-art photography gallery and bookshop with vintage prints by Cartier-Bresson, Bellocq, and more.
places.googleReviewSummary: Photography buffs treasure the museum-quality collection, though it's small and the rarest prints carry serious price tags.
places.localTip: Ask to see the E.J. Bellocq Storyville portraits — haunting images of old New Orleans.
places.story: This two-story gallery holds original prints by photography's giants, including E.J. Bellocq's famous portraits of Storyville's red-light district. It's part museum, part bookshop, and a quiet refuge from the Quarter's noise.

places.name: Mardi Gras World
places.address: 1380 Port of New Orleans Pl, New Orleans, LA 70130
places.reviewSummary: A working warehouse where Mardi Gras floats are built, offering tours among giant sculptures and king cake samples.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love seeing the colossal floats up close and sampling king cake, though it's a working studio so displays vary.
places.localTip: Take the free shuttle from downtown hotels and try on a costume for photos.
places.story: Blaine Kern's studio has built the giant floats for New Orleans parades since 1947, and this warehouse is where the magic gets sculpted year-round. You'll walk among towering papier-mache jesters, dragons, and kings between Carnival seasons.

places.name: Preservation Hall
places.address: 726 St Peter St, New Orleans, LA 70116
places.reviewSummary: The sacred, no-frills room where traditional New Orleans jazz is kept alive nightly by master musicians.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors call the intimate acoustic sets unforgettable, though there's no A/C, limited seating, and lines form early.
places.localTip: Buy a reserved 'big shot' ticket online to skip the line and guarantee a bench seat.
places.story: Since 1961 this candle-lit room with peeling walls has refused microphones, amplifiers, and air conditioning — just acoustic jazz the way the elders played it. The musicians here are living links to the very people who invented jazz a century ago.
places.highlight: Don't miss requesting 'The Saints' (it costs extra, by tradition)

places.name: The Maple Leaf Bar
places.reviewSummary: A pressed-tin Uptown music room famous for the Rebirth Brass Band's legendary Tuesday-night residency.
places.googleReviewSummary: Music lovers call the sweaty brass nights essential, though it's packed, cash-friendly, and ventilation is minimal.
places.localTip: Get there early for Rebirth Brass Band on Tuesdays — it sells out and gets very hot.
places.story: The Maple Leaf has hosted live music nightly since 1974, its walls covered in pressed tin and decades of sweat. The Rebirth Brass Band's Tuesday-night residency here is one of the longest-running and most joyous parties in the city.
places.highlight: Don't miss Rebirth Brass Band on Tuesdays

places.name: Tipitina's
places.reviewSummary: A hallowed Uptown music hall named for a Professor Longhair song, hosting funk, brass, and roots legends since 1977.
places.googleReviewSummary: Concertgoers love the history and acoustics, though sightlines on the floor can be tough when crowded.
places.localTip: Catch the free Sunday Cajun fais do-do dance afternoons when they're scheduled.
places.story: Founded in 1977 to give piano great Professor Longhair a home stage, 'Tip's' is named after one of his songs. A bust of 'Fess' watches over the door, and dancers still rub his head for luck on the way in.

places.name: Snug Harbor Jazz Bistro
places.reviewSummary: Frenchmen Street's premier seated jazz club, showcasing top contemporary and modern jazz in an intimate two-tier room.
places.googleReviewSummary: Jazz fans praise the serious listening-room atmosphere and talent, though it's ticketed and seating is tight.
places.localTip: Reserve seats for the early set, then explore the rest of Frenchmen Street after.
places.story: Snug Harbor is where serious jazz lives on Frenchmen Street, hosting the Marsalis family and the Charmaine Neville Band for decades. Unlike the rowdy bars outside, this is a true listening room where conversation drops when the music starts.

places.name: House of Blues New Orleans
places.reviewSummary: A folk-art-covered concert hall and restaurant hosting national touring acts plus a famous Sunday Gospel Brunch.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests enjoy the eclectic decor and big-name shows, though it's more polished and corporate than the city's grittier clubs.
places.localTip: Book the Sunday Gospel Brunch buffet for soul food and a rousing live choir.
places.story: This venue's walls are crammed with thousands of pieces of Southern folk and outsider art, a deliberate tribute to the roots of the blues. The Sunday Gospel Brunch turns a rock club into a foot-stomping church service.

places.name: Lafitte's Blacksmith Shop Bar
places.reviewSummary: A candle-lit 18th-century cottage said to be the oldest bar in America, famous for its purple frozen 'Voodoo Daiquiri.'
places.googleReviewSummary: Drinkers love the moody candlelit history and signature purple drink, though it's dark, cramped, and cash is easiest.
places.localTip: Go after dark for the full candlelight effect and order the purple 'Voodoo Daiquiri.'
places.story: This crumbling brick-between-posts cottage dates to the 1720s and legend says the pirate Jean Lafitte used it as a front for smuggling. Lit almost entirely by candles, it feels like drinking inside the 18th century.
places.highlight: Don't miss the purple Voodoo Daiquiri

places.name: The Spotted Cat Music Club
places.reviewSummary: A tiny, no-cover Frenchmen Street club packed nightly with trad-jazz and swing bands and impromptu dancing.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the sweaty, authentic live jazz with no cover, though it's small, cash-only, and gets shoulder-to-shoulder.
places.localTip: It's cash-only with a one-drink minimum; arrive early to actually get inside.
places.story: The Spotted Cat is the beating heart of Frenchmen Street, where you squeeze in elbow-to-elbow as a swing band plays a few feet away. There's no cover, no stage to speak of, and often a dancer or two cutting the floor.

places.name: Pat O'Brien's
places.address: 718 St Peter St, New Orleans, LA 70116
places.reviewSummary: The birthplace of the Hurricane cocktail, with a flaming courtyard fountain and a rowdy dueling-piano bar.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests enjoy the iconic Hurricane and lively piano sing-alongs, though the drinks are strong, sweet, and pricey.
places.localTip: Order one Hurricane to keep the souvenir glass, then move to the courtyard with the flaming fountain.
places.story: Pat O'Brien's invented the Hurricane in the 1940s when rum was plentiful but whiskey was scarce, dumping it into a hurricane-lamp-shaped glass. The flagstone courtyard with its fire-and-water fountain remains one of the Quarter's most photographed spots.
places.highlight: Don't miss the flaming courtyard fountain

places.name: Cane & Table
places.reviewSummary: A rustic-chic rum and tiki-leaning cocktail bar with a lush courtyard at the quieter end of the French Quarter.
places.googleReviewSummary: Cocktail fans praise the rum-forward 'proto-tiki' drinks and patio, though it can get busy and seating is limited.
places.localTip: Sit in the back courtyard and order the daiquiri or a rum punch from the rotating menu.
places.story: Cane & Table explores the Caribbean and colonial roots of New Orleans' drinking culture through rum and 'proto-tiki' cocktails. Its crumbling-plaster walls and candlelit courtyard make it feel like a tropical outpost frozen in time.

places.name: Hot Tin (Pontchartrain Hotel)
places.address: 2031 St Charles Ave, New Orleans, LA 70130
places.reviewSummary: A vintage-styled rooftop bar atop the Pontchartrain Hotel with skyline and river views over the Garden District.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests rave about the sunset skyline views and cool decor, though it gets crowded and waits can be long at peak.
places.localTip: Arrive before sunset to claim a spot facing the river and downtown skyline.
places.story: Perched atop the 1927 Pontchartrain Hotel, Hot Tin is decked out like a 1940s artist's loft, a nod to the writers who once lived below. The rooftop offers one of the only elevated views in this low-slung city.
places.highlight: Don't miss the sunset over the Mississippi

places.name: Carousel Bar & Lounge
places.reviewSummary: A slowly rotating, merry-go-round-shaped bar inside the historic Hotel Monteleone, a literary landmark.
places.googleReviewSummary: Patrons love the novelty of the revolving bar and classic cocktails, though seats at the carousel are coveted and the wait is long.
places.localTip: Wait for a seat at the rotating bar itself — it takes about 15 minutes to make a full turn.
places.story: The 25-seat bar slowly revolves a full rotation every fifteen minutes inside the Monteleone, where Hemingway, Faulkner, and Capote all drank. Its hand-painted carousel canopy and circus-animal motifs make every cocktail feel like a ride.

places.name: Erin Rose
places.reviewSummary: A beloved locals' dive just off Bourbon known for frozen Irish coffee and the po'boys from Killer PoBoys in back.
places.googleReviewSummary: Regulars love the frozen Irish coffee and unpretentious vibe, though it's small and packed during service-industry hours.
places.localTip: Order the frozen Irish coffee and a po'boy from the Killer PoBoys kitchen in the back room.
places.story: A block off Bourbon Street's chaos, Erin Rose is where bartenders and locals duck in for a frozen Irish coffee. The back room hides Killer PoBoys, an acclaimed sandwich operation that started as a pop-up in this very bar.
places.highlight: Don't miss the frozen Irish coffee

places.name: Oz New Orleans
places.reviewSummary: A high-energy two-level gay dance club on Bourbon Street with drag shows, go-go dancers, and a balcony over the action.
places.googleReviewSummary: Clubgoers love the dancing, drag, and balcony views, though cover and drink prices climb on weekends.
places.localTip: Head up to the balcony for prime people-watching over the Bourbon-and-St.-Ann gay corner.
places.story: Oz anchors the lively LGBTQ+ stretch of lower Bourbon Street, a corner that's been the heart of gay New Orleans nightlife for decades. Its balcony overlooks the famous intersection where Southern Decadence spills into the streets each Labor Day.

places.name: Maskarade
places.address: 630 St Ann St, New Orleans, LA 70116
places.reviewSummary: A French Quarter shop of handcrafted leather and feather Carnival masks, with mask-making demos and made-to-order pieces.
places.googleReviewSummary: Shoppers admire the artisan quality and one-of-a-kind designs, though fine handmade masks aren't cheap.
places.localTip: Ask the makers about custom commissions and watch them work the leather on-site.
places.story: Maskarade gathers masks from local and international artisans who shape leather, feathers, and porcelain into Carnival art. In a city where masking is a centuries-old tradition, a handmade mask here is a working piece of culture, not a costume-shop trinket.

places.name: Cocktail & Sole (Sazerac House class)
places.reviewSummary: Hands-on cocktail classes at the Sazerac House where you learn to build the Sazerac and other NOLA classics.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love learning to stir a proper Sazerac with expert hosts, though class spots are limited and must be booked ahead.
places.localTip: Reserve the small-group class in advance; it fills faster than the free self-guided tour.
places.story: At the home of the Sazerac, you can learn to make the official cocktail of New Orleans with your own hands. Instructors teach the ritual of the absinthe rinse and the dash of locally born Peychaud's bitters that makes it sing.

places.name: New Orleans GlassWorks (YAYA Arts)
places.reviewSummary: A working glassblowing and printmaking studio in the Arts District offering demos and hands-on hot-glass classes.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors enjoy watching molten glass shaped live and trying it themselves, though hands-on classes book up early.
places.localTip: Book a make-your-own ornament or paperweight session and watch the furnace demo first.
places.story: For decades this Magazine Street studio has been one of the South's largest glassblowing facilities, its furnaces roaring at over 2,000 degrees. You can sign up to blow your own glass beside working artists in the heat of the hot shop.

places.name: Langlois Culinary Crossroads
places.reviewSummary: An intimate cooking-class table where you help prepare and then feast on a multi-course Louisiana meal.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests call it a warm, delicious deep-dive into Creole cooking, though it's pricier and class sizes are small.
places.localTip: Come hungry — the class becomes a full sit-down family-style dinner with wine.
places.story: Chef Amy Sins runs this part-class, part-dinner-party where you cook regional Louisiana dishes and then sit down to eat them together. It's less a lecture than a Creole supper among new friends, with stories about every recipe's roots.

places.name: Royal Street (700 block)
places.reviewSummary: The most photogenic block in the Quarter, lined with lacy iron balconies, galleries, and street musicians.
places.googleReviewSummary: Photographers love the wrought-iron balconies and pastel facades, though midday crowds and harsh light are challenges.
places.localTip: Shoot the corner of Royal and St. Peter early morning for empty streets and soft light.
places.story: The wrought-iron balconies dripping with ferns along Royal Street are the postcard image of New Orleans. On weekends the city closes the street to cars so brass bands and tap dancers can fill it instead.
places.highlight: Don't miss the lacy iron galleries at Royal and St. Peter

places.name: Crescent Park (Rusty Rainbow Bridge)
places.address: 1008 N Peters St, New Orleans, LA 70117
places.reviewSummary: A riverfront park with the photogenic rusted-steel 'Rainbow Bridge' framing skyline and Mississippi views.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the skyline shots and breezy riverside walk, though there's little shade and the arched bridge is steep.
places.localTip: Climb the rusty arched bridge at sunset for the best skyline-and-river photo.
places.story: Crescent Park reclaimed an old industrial wharf into a sleek riverside promenade for the Bywater. Its weathering-steel pedestrian bridge, nicknamed the Rusty Rainbow, frames the downtown skyline across the river like a postcard.
places.highlight: Don't miss the skyline view from the bridge

places.name: Esplanade Avenue Oaks
places.address: Esplanade Ave & N Lopez St, New Orleans, LA 70119
places.reviewSummary: A grand Creole boulevard of antebellum mansions under a canopy of arching live oaks, quieter than St. Charles.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors find it a serene, gorgeous stretch of old NOLA, though it's residential with limited parking.
places.localTip: Walk or bike the stretch toward City Park for the densest oak canopy and best mansions.
places.story: Esplanade was the grand avenue of Creole New Orleans, where wealthy French-speaking families built mansions facing the leafy median. Its tunnel of ancient live oaks makes it one of the most romantic and least crowded streets to photograph.

places.name: Algiers Point Ferry View
places.reviewSummary: Across the river by ferry, this point offers the classic head-on view of the New Orleans skyline and river bend.
places.googleReviewSummary: Riders love the cheap ferry and skyline panorama, though ferry schedules are limited and the point is sleepy.
places.localTip: Ride the Canal Street ferry over at sunset for skyline photos, then explore Algiers Point's cottages.
places.story: A short ferry ride lands you in Algiers Point, a 300-year-old village of Creole cottages on the river's far bank. From its levee you get the only unobstructed, head-on view of the downtown skyline rising above the Mississippi's great curve.
places.highlight: Don't miss the skyline at golden hour

places.name: Garden District (Prytania & First St)
places.address: Prytania St & 1st St, New Orleans, LA 70130
places.reviewSummary: Storybook blocks of antebellum mansions, cast-iron fences, and live oaks, including celebrity homes and Lafayette Cemetery nearby.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors are awed by the grand homes and oak canopy, though it's residential, so be respectful and parking is tight.
places.localTip: Take the St. Charles streetcar to Washington Ave and walk the side streets toward Prytania.
places.story: When Americans poured into New Orleans after the Louisiana Purchase, they built this district of opulent mansions to outshine the Creole Quarter. The cast-iron 'cornstalk' fences and galleried homes have drawn novelists and film crews for generations.

places.name: Bywater Murals (St. Claude Arts)
places.address: 3001 St Claude Ave, New Orleans, LA 70117
places.reviewSummary: The Bywater and St. Claude corridor is covered in vivid murals and street art, a colorful open-air gallery.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the ever-changing, bold street art, though some blocks feel gritty and murals come and go.
places.localTip: Wander the side streets off St. Claude on foot or bike to find the freshest murals.
places.story: The Bywater's weathered shotgun houses and warehouse walls have become canvases for a thriving street-art scene. New murals appear constantly, turning a once-industrial neighborhood into a free, ever-changing outdoor gallery.
